sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Most uncomplicated prehospital births require calm support, preparation, and early recognition of complications rather than active intervention. The main priorities are maternal safety, neonatal warmth and airway support, and rapid escalation when the birth is not progressing normally.

sections.title: Stages of Labour
sections.content:
- Imminent birth is suggested by crowning, an uncontrollable urge to push, bulging perineum, or the presenting part being visible
- If these features are absent, focus on transport, monitoring, and preparation rather than encouraging on-scene delivery
- Be alert for abnormal presentation, multiple birth, heavy bleeding, maternal collapse, or fetal distress concerns

sections.title: Delivery Procedure
sections.content:
1. Position the mother comfortably, preserve privacy, and prepare birth and neonatal equipment

2. Support the head as it crowns, guiding rather than pulling

3. After the head is delivered, check gently for a nuchal cord and manage it if necessary

4. Support delivery of the shoulders and body with controlled guidance only

5. Dry the baby immediately, keep the baby warm, and place skin-to-skin with the mother when appropriate

6. Assess breathing and tone promptly and begin neonatal support if needed

7. Clamp and cut the cord according to local protocol and the baby's condition

8. Manage the third stage conservatively unless active management is indicated by protocol or bleeding risk

sections.title: Neonatal Assessment (APGAR)
sections.content:
- Use APGAR at 1 and 5 minutes as a descriptive handover and documentation tool
- Prioritise practical neonatal assessment: breathing, heart rate, tone, warmth, and response to stimulation
- Do not let APGAR scoring distract from immediate neonatal resuscitation when required

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Avoid traction on the baby's head or neck
- Heavy bleeding, maternal deterioration, retained placenta, or neonatal compromise should trigger rapid escalation and transport priorities
- Keep the baby warm from the moment of birth; hypothermia develops quickly in neonates
- Document delivery time, condition of mother and baby, cord management, placenta status, and any complications
